title: Cybersecurity Advanced II
subtitle: Deeper Threats, Stronger Defences!
overview: Building on Session 5, this advanced session explores deeper cybersecurity topics — network scanning, vulnerability assessment, malware analysis, digital forensics, and incident response. Students think like professional security analysts and practise real defensive techniques used in the industry.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — Nmap scan output, malware lifecycle diagram, incident response flowchart, Kali Linux screenshots, forensics disk image.

keyTopics: Malware Types — Virus, Worm, Trojan, Spyware, Rootkit, Keylogger — how each spreads and harms
Network Scanning — Using Nmap to discover hosts, open ports, and running services
Vulnerability Assessment vs Penetration Testing — Finding vs actively exploiting weaknesses
Incident Response Lifecycle — Prepare → Identify → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Learn
VPN (Virtual Private Network) — Encrypted tunnel that masks IP and secures traffic
Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) — Adding a second layer beyond just a password
Digital Forensics — Collecting, preserving, and analysing digital evidence after an attack
OWASP Top 10 — The ten most critical web application security risks every developer must know

activity.title: Threat Hunt Challenge — Malware Identification + Incident Response Simulation
parts: Printed Incident Response scenario cards (3 scenarios)
Whiteboard for drawing the IR lifecycle
TryHackMe free account (optional for advanced challenge)
steps: Part 1 — Malware ID: Visit VirusTotal and upload a safe dummy file (or paste a known malicious URL provided by teacher)
Read the scan report — identify which antivirus engines flagged it and what malware family it belongs to
Discuss: What type of malware is it? How does it spread? What damage does it cause?
Part 2 — Incident Response Simulation: Draw the IR Lifecycle on the whiteboard — Prepare, Identify, Contain, Eradicate, Recover, Learn
Each team draws an Incident Response scenario card (e.g., 'Ransomware detected on 10 school computers')
Teams must walk through all 6 IR steps for their scenario and present to the class
Part 3 — 2FA Demo: Show how enabling 2FA on a Google account works — demonstrate authenticator app vs SMS code
Discuss: Why is SMS-based 2FA weaker than an authenticator app?
Challenge: Complete TryHackMe's free 'Intro to Cybersecurity' room for hands-on practice!

quiz.q: What is the difference between a VIRUS and a WORM?
options: A virus needs a host file to attach to and spread; a worm spreads independently across networks without needing a host file
A worm needs a host file; a virus spreads on its own
They are exactly the same thing
A virus only attacks hardware; a worm only attacks software
quiz.explanation: Virus = attaches to a file and spreads when that file is shared. Worm = self-replicating, travels across networks on its own — no host file needed. Worms spread much faster!

quiz.q: What is a TROJAN HORSE in cybersecurity?
options: A physical device hidden inside a computer
Malware disguised as legitimate software that secretly performs malicious actions once installed
A type of DDoS attack using fake IP addresses
An ancient encryption algorithm
quiz.explanation: Trojan = disguised malware! Like the ancient Trojan Horse — it looks like a useful app (game, tool, software) but secretly installs malware, steals data, or gives attackers remote access.

options: Locks your keyboard so you cannot type
Records every keystroke you type — capturing passwords, messages, and credit card numbers
Speeds up keyboard input
Encrypts your files and demands ransom
quiz.explanation: Keylogger = silent spy! It records EVERYTHING you type — passwords, bank details, messages — and sends it to the attacker. You never know it's there until it's too late!

options: A tool used to fix corrupted system files
Malware that hides deep inside the operating system, giving attackers persistent hidden access with admin privileges
A network scanning tool used by ethical hackers
A type of firewall that operates at the root level
quiz.explanation: Rootkit = the sneakiest malware! It hides so deep in the OS that antivirus tools often cannot detect it. It gives attackers ROOT (admin) access — full control of your system, silently.

options: Creating encrypted tunnels for VPN connections
Scanning networks to discover connected hosts, open ports, and running services
Analysing malware files in a sandbox
Recovering deleted files from a hard drive
quiz.explanation: Nmap = Network Mapper! It scans a network to find: which devices are online, which ports are open, and what services are running. Used by both attackers (reconnaissance) and defenders (auditing)!

quiz.q: What is the difference between VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT and PENETRATION TESTING?
options: They are the same process with different names
Vulnerability assessment only scans and identifies weaknesses; penetration testing actively exploits those weaknesses to prove the impact
Penetration testing only scans; vulnerability assessment actively exploits
Vulnerability assessment is illegal; penetration testing is always legal
quiz.explanation: VA = finding weaknesses (like checking if doors are unlocked). Pen Testing = actually opening the door to prove it works (with permission!). Pen testing goes deeper and shows real business impact.

quiz.q: What is the FIRST step in the Incident Response lifecycle?
options: Contain
Eradicate
Prepare
Identify
quiz.explanation: Prepare comes FIRST! Before any attack happens, you must have policies, tools, and a trained team ready. The full cycle: Prepare → Identify → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Lessons Learned.

quiz.q: During an incident, what does CONTAINMENT mean?
options: Deleting all evidence of the attack
Paying the ransom to restore files quickly
Isolating affected systems to stop the attack from spreading further
Rebuilding all systems from scratch
quiz.explanation: Containment = stop the bleeding! Disconnect infected machines from the network immediately to prevent the attack from spreading to other systems. Preserve evidence — don't wipe anything yet!

quiz.q: What is a VPN (Virtual Private Network)?
options: A private social network for company employees
An encrypted tunnel between your device and the internet that hides your IP address and secures your traffic
A type of firewall that blocks all incoming connections
A tool for scanning Wi-Fi networks
quiz.explanation: VPN = encrypted tunnel! All your internet traffic goes through an encrypted tunnel to a VPN server — websites see the VPN's IP, not yours. Protects against MITM on public Wi-Fi!

quiz.q: Why is an AUTHENTICATOR APP safer than SMS for Two-Factor Authentication?
options: Authenticator apps are easier to use
SMS codes are longer and harder to type
SMS can be intercepted via SIM swapping or SS7 attacks; authenticator app codes are generated locally and never transmitted
Authenticator apps never expire their codes
quiz.explanation: SMS 2FA can be hijacked by SIM swapping (tricking your carrier to transfer your number) or SS7 network attacks. Authenticator app codes are generated offline on your device — never sent over a network!

options: Designing secure digital systems
The process of collecting, preserving, and analysing digital evidence from devices after a security incident
Testing software for bugs before release
Monitoring network traffic in real time
quiz.explanation: Digital Forensics = cyber detective work! After an attack, forensic analysts examine hard drives, logs, and memory to find what happened, who did it, and how — preserving evidence for legal proceedings.

options: Online Web Application Security Protocol
Open Worldwide Application Security Project
Organised Web Attack and Safety Procedures
Official Web Auditing and Scanning Platform
quiz.explanation: OWASP = Open Worldwide Application Security Project! A non-profit that publishes the famous OWASP Top 10 — the most critical web security risks every developer must know and fix.

quiz.q: Which of these is in the OWASP Top 10?
options: Slow internet connection
Broken Access Control
Outdated graphics drivers
Weak Wi-Fi signal
quiz.explanation: Broken Access Control is #1 on the OWASP Top 10! It means users can access data or functions they shouldn't — like a regular user accessing admin pages. A massive and common vulnerability!

options: It creates viruses for research purposes
It scans files and URLs against 70+ antivirus engines simultaneously to detect malware
It monitors network traffic for intrusions
It recovers files deleted by ransomware
quiz.explanation: VirusTotal = free malware scanner! Upload a suspicious file or URL and it checks it against 70+ antivirus engines at once. If multiple engines flag it — it's almost certainly malware!

options: A network of robots used in manufacturing
A group of security researchers who fight cybercrime
A network of malware-infected computers controlled remotely by an attacker to perform coordinated attacks
A type of VPN service
quiz.explanation: Botnet = army of zombie computers! Malware silently infects thousands of devices — they become 'bots'. The attacker commands them all at once — commonly used for DDoS attacks and spam campaigns!
